question: Define what it means for a set to be countable. Provide a brief, intuitive explanation of this concept.
expectedPoints: A set is countable if it is finite or if it can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the set of natural numbers.
This means all elements of the set can be listed in a sequence (even if the sequence is infinite).
The concept establishes a 'size' or cardinality for infinite sets, with the set of natural numbers (ℵ₀) being the smallest infinity.

question: Prove that the set of all integers, ℤ, is countable.
expectedPoints: To prove countability, one must demonstrate a bijection between ℤ and ℕ.
A common proof defines the function f: ℕ → ℤ where f(1)=0, f(2)=1, f(3)=-1, f(4)=2, f(5)=-2, and so on.
This function explicitly lists every integer, proving that ℤ can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with ℕ and is therefore countable.

question: State and prove Cantor's Theorem. Explain its profound significance in the theory of metric spaces and the concept of cardinality.
expectedPoints: Cantor's Theorem: For any set X, the cardinality of its power set P(X) is strictly greater than the cardinality of X itself (|P(X)| > |X|).

The proof involves showing no surjection exists from X to P(X). Assuming a surjection f: X → P(X) exists, one constructs the set D = {x ∈ X : x ∉ f(x)}. Since D ∈ P(X), there must be some d ∈ X such that f(d) = D. This leads to the contradiction: d ∈ f(d) if and only if d ∉ f(d).

Significance: It proves there are infinitely many levels of infinity (e.g., |ℕ| < |P(ℕ)| = |ℝ|). In metric spaces, it shows that although ℝ is uncountable, its cardinality (c) is just one level above the countable infinity of ℕ (ℵ₀).
